skill.name: when-training-neural-networks-use-flow-nexus-neural
skill.description: Guides advanced users through training, validating, and deploying neural networks with Flow Nexus, E2B sandboxes, Claude Flow hooks, and Docker-based serving.
skill.summary: Coordinate distributed neural network training and deployment with Flow Nexus.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many blocker patterns, but the weak-cryptography and reconnaissance matches are mostly false positives from prose, diagram labels, and ML terms. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill instructs agents to run external CLI commands, authenticate to Flow Nexus, create deployment scripts, publish templates, and start a Docker service, so publication should include a clear execution warning.

medium_findings.title: Operational Commands Can Modify Local and Cloud State
medium_findings.description: The skill is a runbook that instructs agents to run npx commands, create directories and files, chmod scripts, call Flow Nexus MCP tools, and execute a generated deployment script. This is legitimate for the stated ML workflow, but it can change the local workspace and external Flow Nexus resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly instruct command execution and file creation. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: External Platform Authentication and Publishing Workflow
medium_findings.description: The workflow includes Flow Nexus authentication, cluster initialization, node deployment, distributed training, and optional template publishing. These actions may handle credentials, consume cloud resources, or publish model metadata outside the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Flow Nexus calls and login examples are explicit. I did not find evidence of credential exfiltration, but the workflow clearly involves external authenticated operations.

medium_findings.title: Generated Docker Service Binds to All Interfaces
medium_findings.description: The deployment instructions generate a Dockerfile that runs uvicorn on 0.0.0.0 and a shell script that starts a container mapped to local port 8000. This is expected for serving a model, but it can expose a service if run on a shared or public host.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Docker command and 0.0.0.0 binding are directly present. The risk depends on the host network context, so confidence is high for exposure potential but not for malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography matches occur in prose, diagram labels, and machine-learning words such as design, description, dense, and transformer configuration. I did not find evidence that the skill implements MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are natural-language workflow text or ML architecture examples. No cryptographic API usage or hash implementation was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Local Test Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to localhost health and metrics endpoints plus reference links for Flow Nexus, E2B, Claude Flow, and TensorFlow Serving. These are not evidence of covert network exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visibly used for local deployment tests and documentation references. No sensitive data is sent to those URLs in the reviewed lines.

low_findings.title: Memory Label Triggered Environment Access Finding
low_findings.description: The env_access finding in the Graphviz file is a diagram note for workflow memory keys, not process environment access. I did not find evidence that this file reads environment variables or secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location is a Graphviz label that documents memory coordination points. It is not executable code and contains no process.env or shell environment expansion.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party CLI Invocation in Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs use of npx claude-flow@alpha hooks and memory commands. This can execute package code from the user environment when followed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and numerous. They are part of the documented workflow, but users should approve third-party CLI execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Generation and Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow creates executable shell scripts for checkpoint backup and Docker deployment, then tells users to run the deployment script.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script bodies and chmod commands are directly shown. The scripts are understandable and relevant, but they alter local runtime state.

dangerous_patterns.title: External MCP Operations with Credentials
dangerous_patterns.description: The runbook includes Flow Nexus login examples and authenticated MCP actions for cluster creation, node deployment, training, and publishing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly depends on authenticated Flow Nexus operations. The credentials shown are placeholders, so I did not classify this as credential theft.

content.user_title: Train Neural Networks with Flow Nexus
content.value_statement: Distributed neural network training requires coordinated setup, validation, deployment, and monitoring. This skill provides a structured Flow Nexus workflow for moving from model design to a served API.
actual_capabilities: Outlines setup steps for Flow Nexus authentication and neural cluster initialization.
Provides staged guidance for architecture design, node deployment, and distributed training.
Includes validation, benchmarking, and performance reporting checkpoints.
Describes model packaging with Docker, FastAPI, health checks, and metrics endpoints.
Uses Claude Flow memory and hooks to coordinate progress across workflow phases.
limitations: Requires a working Flow Nexus MCP server and valid platform credentials.
Uses example model configuration and placeholder metrics that must be adapted.
May run external commands and create local files when followed by an agent.
Does not guarantee model accuracy, cloud availability, or production security hardening.

use_cases.title: Prototype distributed model training
use_cases.description: Plan a Flow Nexus training cluster, select an architecture, and define basic training parameters for a new model.

use_cases.title: Prepare model validation workflow
use_cases.description: Create checkpoints for accuracy, latency, convergence, benchmarking, and performance reporting before deployment.

use_cases.title: Package a model serving endpoint
use_cases.description: Generate deployment steps for Docker, FastAPI serving, health checks, metrics, and basic operational documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a training run
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new model workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan a Flow Nexus neural network training run for my dataset. Include prerequisites, phases, and approval points before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Configure architecture and training
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a transformer training configuration for a classification task. Explain which values I should customize for my dataset.

prompt_templates.title: Validate model readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a validation checklist for my trained model. Cover accuracy, latency, overfitting, benchmarks, and deployment readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Design an MLOps deployment flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an end-to-end Flow Nexus training and Docker deployment workflow. Mark every external command and cloud action for manual approval.

output_examples.input: I need to train a classifier with Flow Nexus and deploy it for testing.
output: A five-phase training plan from authentication through deployment.
A list of required credentials, tools, and resource assumptions.
Validation checkpoints for accuracy, latency, and health checks.

output_examples.input: Review my model before I publish it as a Flow Nexus template.
output: A readiness review covering metrics, documentation, and rollback concerns.
Warnings about authenticated publishing and external resource usage.
Recommended manual approval steps before publication.

output_examples.input: Prepare a local inference service for a trained model.
output: A Docker-based serving outline with health and metrics endpoints.
Operational notes about port exposure and resource limits.
A testing checklist for local prediction and monitoring requests.

best_practices: Review every generated command before execution, especially npx, Docker, and MCP operations.
Use dedicated test credentials and isolated Flow Nexus resources for experiments.
Replace placeholder metrics, passwords, ports, and model settings before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not run the full workflow in a production workspace without reviewing generated files.
Do not publish model templates until ownership, licensing, and validation results are clear.
Do not expose the generated API on a public host without authentication and network controls.

faq.question: Does this skill train a model by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides a workflow that an agent or user can follow with Flow Nexus and supporting tools.

faq.question: Which tools are required?
faq.answer: It expects Flow Nexus MCP access. Docker, Claude Flow hooks, and E2B resources are used by parts of the workflow.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, or Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is it safe to run automatically?
faq.answer: No. The workflow includes external commands, authenticated cloud actions, and Docker deployment steps that need review.

faq.question: Does it include production security controls?
faq.answer: Only basic deployment guidance is included. Add authentication, network restrictions, secrets management, and monitoring before production use.

faq.question: What should I customize first?
faq.answer: Start with dataset details, architecture settings, training thresholds, credentials, deployment ports, and resource limits.
